critique.summary: The article discusses Illinois House Bill 5511, known as the Children's Social Media Safety Act, which mandates operating system providers to verify users' ages by January 1, 2028. The bill requires age verification at account setup and imposes default settings for minors, with violations considered unlawful under the Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act.
mainPoints: HB5511 proposes the Children's Social Media Safety Act to enhance age verification on operating systems.
Operating system providers must require users to provide birth date or age during account setup by January 1, 2028.
Operators must send age category signals upon request and only share minimal necessary information.
Platforms cannot operate in Illinois without conducting required age verification to identify minors.
Default settings must be applied for users known to be minors.
Violations of the Act are classified as unlawful practices under existing consumer protection laws.
The bill was introduced by Rep. Jennifer Gong-Gershowitz and has multiple co-sponsors.
The bill was assigned to the Judiciary - Civil Committee as of March 4, 2026.
critique.truthfulness: The information provided aligns with the official Illinois General Assembly bill status page for HB5511, confirming the bill's introduction, sponsors, committee assignment, and key provisions. The bill's requirements and timeline are accurately represented. However, the article does not cite multiple sources or perspectives beyond the legislative text, limiting the depth of context.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential challenges or criticisms of the bill from privacy advocates or technology companies.
There is no mention of how the age verification process will be implemented technically or its impact on user privacy.
The article does not address enforcement mechanisms or penalties beyond stating violations are unlawful.
Broader implications for social media platforms and operating system providers outside Illinois are not explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Illinois' proposed bill requiring operating systems to include age verification features, with many commenters expressing skepticism about its effectiveness and motivations. Some highlight Meta's lobbying efforts to shift liability, while others question the practicality and enforcement of such legislation, especially concerning open-source OSes.

comments.author: 0xbadcafebee
comments.text:
> the Children's Social Media Safety Act
  > 
  > provide an accessible interface at account setup that requires an account holder to indicate the birth date, age, or both
Thank goodness kids can't lie about their age!

  > provide an operator who has requested a signal with respect to a particular user a signal that identifies the user's age by category
Wait - if this is just to pass a signal to an operator ("social media site"), why can't the "operator" just ask for the age themselves?

Answer: they don't want to be liable and get fined $400 Million, like Meta got fined, for letting kids on social media. (https://www.nytimes.com/2022/09/05/business/meta-children-da...)

This is why Meta is forcing this legislation through nation-wide. They are forcing Google/Apple to take the liability, despite it not actually being Google or Apple that's providing the "harmful" social media. Meta are doing this state-by-state so nobody can track that it's them. Easier than pushing at a federal level, and raises fewer red flags from news media.

Since Google and Apple won't want to accept this liability either, the next step is requiring digital IDs and third-party verification to prove the user is of age. This will enable tracking of all users, whatever app or website they go to. Bills requiring this are already being passed at state and federal level.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-reasoned explanation of Meta's motivations and the broader implications of the bill, supported by references to real-world events.

comments.author: glitchc
comments.text: The law as is written mainly targets social media platforms. For an OS to comply, all it needs to do is provide a field during account creation that records the user's date of birth as supplied by the user. There is no onus on the operator to confirm the veracity of this information, or even record it anywhere other than the local OS install itself. I think we're safe.
comments.analysis: Accurately summarizes the bill's requirements and clarifies that OS operators have minimal verification responsibilities, reducing concerns about heavy enforcement.

comments.author: mikestorrent
comments.text:
I don't like this whole thing, but compared to what I was fearing would happen, this idea is nowhere near as bad.

What I expected was that we'd end up with the OS vendors actually being mandated to really do age verification, and then submitting that using Web Credentials and Secure Attestation so that the far end could trust the whole thing, locking open-source OS's out of the mix and creating more of a walled garden online than we already have. I was guessing it would become a simple checkbox on e.g. Cloudflare - "[ ] allow adult users only" or whatever - and that it would end up with vast swathes of the internet going off limits for anyone not on closed-source systems.

Now, it looks like this is just a way for parents to tell the OS "this is a kid account" and have it flow through to websites so they can easily proactively block kids from connecting without having to implement any of that crap. Yes, it's much potentially easier for a child to circumvent; but any kid who can get around that sort of thing from within an OS could probably just wipe/reinstall anyway, so who cares?

As a parent whose kids are continually trying to see what trouble they can get into, I appreciate that I will get one more potential weapon in the fight.

Can someone tell me whether I am being a fool by actually being a bit relieved it's going this way?
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced perspective on the bill's practical impact, acknowledging limitations but appreciating the potential parental control benefits.

comments.author: Slow_Hand
comments.text:
If I’m not mistaken, Meta has been lobbying heavily for all of these age-verification bills lately.

It seems their strategy is to externalize their responsibility to verify age themselves, and thus reduce their exposure to liabilities when child protection acts like COPPA are violated.
comments.analysis: Correctly identifies Meta's lobbying efforts and strategic motivations behind the legislation, aligning with other informed comments.

comments.author: saityi
comments.text:
Even if open source operating systems comply and add such a feature, what's to stop individual people from removing this and blocking the API requests before they install the OS? Or providing dummy responses? They're open source, after all.

Is the government going to require some sort of automated checks that verify every person who connects to the internet has this API on their OS and go after individuals that aren't in compliance?
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about the enforceability of the legislation on open-source systems and individual compliance.

comments.author: rnxrx
comments.text: There's something I've never seen a good answer to: why is this being mandated in the OS vs requiring it for apps - or classes of apps?  There's plenty of parental controls already available for browsers - after verifying the user's age on startup, why not add a header field that the browser inserts along with AgentID (for example) and call it a day?
comments.analysis: Provides a reasonable critique of the bill's scope and suggests alternative approaches that might be more effective or less intrusive.

comments.author: firtoz
comments.text:
It's kinda convenient because every service needs to ask you for the age now because they can't serve under 13s in a lot of cases. Having it be a simple API would be a decent convenience, no?

If you connect it with a permission system where you can choose whether to provide this information (e.g. >13 as a bool or age as an integer or the birthday as a date) that can't be too bad I guess?

I haven't read the whole thing of course.
comments.analysis: Offers a constructive idea for implementing age verification that respects user choice and privacy, though admits limited familiarity with the bill.

comments.author: ottah
comments.text: This coordinated state level attack on the legislative process is crazy. These people can't seem to be bothered to do the basics of governing, but they always find time to do this cross-state nightmare fuel.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration but lacks substantive argument or evidence, making the comment more emotional than analytical.

comments.author: hypeatei
comments.text: What are they going to do to enforce this? Take down open source projects that "operate" in Illinois because a user downloaded the software there? Absolute joke and everyone should treat it that way; advanced compliance here means implicit support for the surveillance state.
comments.analysis: Raises important civil liberties concerns, though the claim about implicit surveillance support is speculative without direct evidence.

comments.author: fhn
comments.text: These people are just so clueless. All they will find is that everybody on the internet is an adult.
comments.analysis: Highlights a common practical limitation of age verification but does so in a dismissive tone without deeper analysis.
